title: Beer Color SRM Calculator Morey 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Your Beer's SRM Color with the Morey Formula
description: Calculate beer color SRM with the Morey formula. Enter malt weight, lovibond degrees, and final volume. Pale Ale 5-10 SRM, IPA 6-14, Stout 30+ SRM.
intro: The **SRM (Standard Reference Method)** measures beer color on a scale from 1 (pale straw) to 40+ (black). The **Morey formula (1993)** predicts final SRM from the MCU (Malt Color Units) of each malt. Enter the total weight of base malts, crystal malts, and roasted malts with their lovibond degrees, and get the final SRM to validate your recipe against BJCP style guidelines.
keyTakeaway: **Morey: SRM = 1.4922 × MCU^0.6859**. Pale Ale 5-10 SRM, IPA 6-14, Stout 30-40+ SRM.
useCases: You're brewing a recipe and want to match your style's exact color.
You added crystal malt and want to see how it affects the final color.
You're cloning a commercial beer and need to match its color profile.
You switched malt suppliers and the lovibond degrees differ from your recipe.
You're entering a competition and need to hit your style's SRM range.

example.title: Example: American Amber Ale
steps: **Malts**: 5 kg Pale 2L + 0.5 kg Crystal 60L + 0.2 kg Chocolate 350L.
example.result: Color matches American Amber to Brown Ale.

explanation:
## What is SRM

The **SRM (Standard Reference Method)** is the American beer color scale, measured at 430 nm. It runs from 1 (pale straw) to 40+ (black). In Europe, **EBC = SRM × 1.97**.

## Morey Formula

```
MCU = Σ(kg × °L × 2.205) / volume_gallons
SRM = 1.4922 × MCU^0.6859
```

It's non-linear: at higher MCU, marginal contribution decreases. That's why an extra 100g of chocolate in a stout doesn't shift the color much.

## SRM by Beer Style

| Style | SRM | Color |
|---|---|---|
| Light Lager | 2-4 | Pale straw |
| Witbier | 2-4 | Cloudy white |
| Hefeweizen | 3-9 | Yellow-amber |
| **Pale Ale** | 5-10 | Golden |
| **IPA** | 6-14 | Golden-copper |
| Amber Ale | 10-17 | Amber |
| Brown Ale | 18-35 | Brown |
| Porter | 22-40 | Dark brown |
| **Stout** | 30-40+ | Black |

## Typical Malt Lovibond Ratings

| Malt | °L |
|---|---|
| Pilsner | 1.5-2 |
| Pale 2-row | 2 |
| Munich | 8-10 |
| Vienna | 4 |
| Crystal 60 | 60 |
| Crystal 120 | 120 |
| Chocolate | 300-400 |
| Black Patent | 500 |
| Roasted Barley | 300-500 |

## Common Mistakes

1. **Ignoring the boil**: Maillard reactions add 1-2 SRM in long boils.
2. **Confusing Lovibond with EBC**: Different scales entirely.
3. **Using Mosher instead**: Overstates dark malts; Morey is more accurate.

See [ABV Calculator](/calculadora-abv-cerveza-og-fg) to complete your recipe.

faq.a: SRM stands for Standard Reference Method, the American standard for measuring beer color at 430 nanometers.
Morey fits log-linear to thousands of real beers and is more accurate than linear Mosher. It's the industry standard in BeerSmith.

faq.q: How do I convert SRM to EBC?
faq.a: Multiply SRM by 1.97. Example: 7 SRM Pale Ale = 13.8 EBC.
Yes. Long boils (90+ minutes) cause Maillard browning, adding 1-3 SRM. Account for this by reducing MCU by about 1 SRM in your calculations.

faq.q: How much color do different malts add?
faq.a: Base malts: 2-4°L. Crystal malts: 20-200°L. Roasted/chocolate: 300-500°L. See the malt table in the explanation above.

faq.q: What if my beer came out darker than predicted?
faq.a: Long boil time, low mash pH, or oxidation can darken beer. Try covering the pot partially during brewing and controlling water chemistry.

faq.q: Can I toast malts at home?
faq.a: Home toasting is inconsistent. Use commercial malts with known lovibond ratings to get repeatable results in your recipes.

faq.q: Is there a beer color chart I can reference?
faq.a: Yes. Pale Ale (golden 5-10 SRM), IPA (golden-copper 6-14), Porter (dark brown 22-40), Stout (black 30-40+).
